summary: Joel argues that as companies grow and employees specialize, managers often misdiagnose their communication problems. They think they need more coordination and alignment meetings, when the real issue is that too many people are inserting themselves into decisions that already have capable owners. The post is a brief pointer to his Inc. magazine column expanding on this idea.
key_points: Company growth leads to specialization, which creates perceived communication gaps
Managers often misdiagnose the problem as needing more alignment when they actually need less interference
Having smart people already working on something means others should step back, not pile on
money_quotes: As companies expand, the people within them start to specialize.
Some managers will conclude that they have a 'keep everyone on the same page' problem.
But often what they actually have is a 'stop people from meddling when there are already enough smart people working on something' problem.
key_insight: The real scaling problem in growing companies isn't insufficient communication — it's too many people meddling in work that already has competent owners.
